content.text: MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT
Draft v2 — For Review

This Master Services Agreement ("Agreement") is entered into as of March 1, 2026 (the "Effective Date"), by and between Apex Dynamics Inc., a Delaware corporation ("Client"), and Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC, a Virginia limited liability company ("Provider").
RECITALS
WHEREAS, Client desires to engage Provider to deliver managed cloud infrastructure services; and
WHEREAS, Provider has the expertise, personnel, and resources necessary to perform such services;
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, the parties agree as follows:
Article 1 — Definitions

"Confidential Information" means any non-public information disclosed by either party to the other, whether in written, oral, or electronic form, that is designated as confidential or that a reasonable person would understand to be confidential given the nature of the information and circumstances of disclosure.
"Services" means the managed cloud infrastructure services described in Article 2 and any Statements of Work executed under this Agreement.
"Deliverables" means all work product, reports, documentation, and materials produced by Provider in connection with the Services.
Article 2 — Scope of Services

2.1  Provider shall deliver the following managed cloud infrastructure services: (a) provisioning, configuration, and management of cloud compute, storage, and networking resources on AWS and Azure; (b) 24/7 monitoring, incident detection, and response; (c) monthly capacity planning and optimization reports; (d) disaster recovery architecture with agreed RPO and RTO targets; and (e) quarterly business reviews.
2.2  The parties may supplement this Agreement with one or more Statements of Work, each of which shall be governed by the terms of this Agreement.
Article 3 — Term and Renewal
3.1  This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for a period of three (3) years (the "Initial Term").

3.2  Upon expiration of the Initial Term, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one (1) year periods (each a "Renewal Term") unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current term.
Article 4 — Fees and Payment
4.1  Client shall pay Provider a monthly service fee of Forty-Five Thousand Dollars ($45,000.00), payable in arrears.
4.2  Invoices are due and payable within forty-five (45) days of receipt ("Net 45"). Late payments shall accrue interest at the lesser of 1.5% per month or the maximum rate permitted by law.
4.3  Provider may increase fees upon sixty (60) days' written notice, provided that any increase shall not exceed 5% per annum.
Article 5 — Service Level Agreement
5.1  Provider guarantees a monthly uptime of 99.9% for all production environments.

5.2  In the event Provider fails to meet the uptime target, Client shall be entitled to service credits equal to 5% of the monthly fee for each 0.05% shortfall below the target, up to a maximum credit of 20% of the monthly fee.
5.3  Service credits are Client's sole and exclusive remedy for Provider's failure to meet the uptime commitment set forth in Section 5.1.
Article 6 — Confidentiality and Data Protection
6.1  Each party agrees to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose it to any third party without prior written consent, except as required by law.
6.2  Provider shall encrypt all Client data at rest using AES-256 encryption and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
6.3  Provider shall implement commercially reasonable security controls consistent with industry best practices for cloud service providers.
6.4  Provider shall not transfer Client data outside the continental United States without prior written consent.
Article 7 — Intellectual Property

7.1  Provider shall retain all right, title, and interest in and to any tools, scripts, configurations, and methodologies developed in connection with the Services, whether pre-existing or newly created. Client is granted a non-exclusive, perpetual license to use such materials solely in connection with Client's internal operations.
7.2  Client retains all right, title, and interest in Client's pre-existing intellectual property and data.
Article 8 — Limitation of Liability
8.1  EXCEPT FOR BREACHES OF ARTICLE 6 (CONFIDENTIALITY), NEITHER PARTY'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT SHALL EXCEED AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO SIX (6) MONTHS OF FEES PAID OR PAYABLE HEREUNDER.
8.2  IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, REGARDLESS OF THE CAUSE OF ACTION OR THEORY OF LIABILITY.
Article 9 — Termination
9.1  Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience by providing sixty (60) days' prior written notice to the other party.
9.2  Either party may terminate this Agreement for cause upon thirty (30) days' written notice if the other party commits a material breach and fails to cure such breach within the notice period.

9.3  Upon termination, Provider shall (a) cease performance of the Services, (b) return or destroy all Client Confidential Information, and (c) cooperate in good faith with any transition activities for a period of up to ninety (90) days, at Client's expense.
Article 10 — Governing Law and Dispute Resolution
10.1  This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.

10.2  Any dispute arising under this Agreement shall first be submitted to good-faith mediation. If mediation fails to resolve the dispute within sixty (60) days, either party may initiate binding arbitration in New York, New York under the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
Article 11 — General Provisions

11.1  Entire Agreement.  This Agreement, together with any Statements of Work and exhibits, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous understandings.
11.2  Amendment.  This Agreement may be amended only by a written instrument signed by both parties.
11.3  Waiver.  The failure of either party to enforce any right under this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of that right.
11.4  Severability.  If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid, the remaining provisions shall continue in full force and effect.
11.5  Notices.  All notices under this Agreement shall be in writing and delivered by certified mail, overnight courier, or email with confirmed receipt to the addresses set forth in this Agreement.

11.6  Assignment.  Neither party may assign this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other party, except in connection with a merger, acquisition, or sale of substantially all of the assigning party's assets.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF
The parties have executed this Master Services Agreement as of the Effective Date first written above.
APEX DYNAMICS INC.
Name: Catherine A. Morales
Title: Chief Operating Officer
STRATOS CLOUD SOLUTIONS LLC
Name: David R. Thornton
Title: Managing Director
EXECUTED TERM SHEET
Managed Cloud Infrastructure Services
CONFIDENTIAL
1. Parties
Client: Apex Dynamics Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal office at 200 Innovation Drive, Suite 800, Wilmington, DE 19801 ("Client" or "Apex").
Provider: Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC, a Virginia limited liability company with its principal office at 1455 Reston Parkway, Suite 320, Reston, VA 20190 ("Provider" or "Stratos").
2. Effective Date
March 1, 2026.
3. Scope of Services

Provider shall deliver managed cloud infrastructure services to Client, including: (a) provisioning, configuration, and management of cloud compute, storage, and networking resources on AWS and Azure; (b) 24/7 monitoring, incident detection, and response; (c) monthly capacity planning and optimization reports; (d) disaster recovery architecture with a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 1 hour and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 4 hours; and (e) quarterly business reviews with Client's IT leadership team.
4. Term
Three (3) years from the Effective Date, expiring February 28, 2029, unless earlier terminated in accordance with Section 10.
5. Fees and Payment
Monthly Service Fee: Forty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($47,500.00) per month, payable in arrears.
Payment Terms: Net Thirty (30) days from receipt of a valid invoice. Late payments shall accrue interest at 1.5% per month.
6. Service Level Agreement

Provider guarantees a monthly uptime of 99.95% for all production environments. Failure to meet this target entitles Client to service credits equal to 5% of the monthly fee for each 0.01% below 99.95%, up to a maximum credit of 30% of the monthly fee.
7. Data Handling and Compliance

Provider shall maintain SOC 2 Type II certification throughout the Term and provide annual audit reports to Client within thirty (30) days of report issuance. Provider shall encrypt all Client data at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+). Provider shall not transfer Client data outside the continental United States without prior written consent.
8. Intellectual Property

All custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, and derivative works created by Provider specifically for Client under this engagement ("Custom Work Product") shall be the exclusive property of Client. Provider retains ownership of its pre-existing tools, frameworks, and methodologies.
9. Limitation of Liability

Each party's aggregate liability under this agreement shall not exceed an amount equal to twelve (12) months of fees paid or payable under this Term Sheet (the "Liability Cap"). This cap does not apply to breaches of confidentiality obligations, willful misconduct, or indemnification obligations arising from third-party IP claims.
10. Termination

Either party may terminate this agreement for convenience by providing ninety (90) days' prior written notice to the other party. Either party may terminate for cause upon thirty (30) days' written notice if the other party materially breaches this agreement and fails to cure such breach within the notice period.
11. Governing Law and Dispute Resolution

This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. Any dispute arising under this agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware under the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
12. Signatures
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Term Sheet as of the Effective Date.
APEX DYNAMICS INC.
By: /s/ Catherine A. Morales
Name: Catherine A. Morales
Title: Chief Operating Officer
Date: March 1, 2026
STRATOS CLOUD SOLUTIONS LLC
By: /s/ David R. Thornton
Name: David R. Thornton
Title: Managing Director
Date: March 1, 2026
CONTRACT REVIEW MEMORANDUM
TO:       Catherine A. Morales, COO — Apex Dynamics Inc.
FROM:     Outside Counsel — Contract Review Team
DATE:     March 16, 2026
RE:       Review of Draft MSA v2 — Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC
1. Executive Summary

We have completed a line-by-line comparison of the executed Term Sheet dated March 1, 2026, against Draft MSA v2 circulated by Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC. Our review identified nine (9) discrepancies between the two documents, of which three are classified as Critical, three as High, two as Medium, and one as Low severity. The most significant issues involve the monthly service fee, intellectual property ownership, and the liability cap — all of which deviate materially from the agreed commercial terms. We strongly recommend that these discrepancies be resolved before the MSA is executed.
2. Discrepancy Summary Table
3. Detailed Analysis
3.1  Monthly Service Fee (Article 4.1) — CRITICAL

The Term Sheet sets the monthly service fee at $47,500.00. The draft MSA states $45,000.00, a $2,500 per-month shortfall that amounts to $90,000 over the three-year term. This is almost certainly a drafting error, but if left uncorrected it would bind Client to a higher payment than negotiated or create ambiguity that Provider could exploit.
Recommended language:
"Client shall pay Provider a monthly service fee of Forty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($47,500.00), payable in arrears."
3.2  Payment Terms (Article 4.2) — CRITICAL

The Term Sheet specifies Net 30 payment terms. The draft MSA extends this to Net 45. While a longer payment window may appear to benefit Client, the inconsistency creates a contract-interpretation risk. More importantly, the late-payment interest provision could trigger unexpectedly if Client's accounts payable processes are calibrated to the Net 30 cycle agreed in the Term Sheet.
Recommended language:
"Invoices are due and payable within thirty (30) days of receipt ("Net 30"). Late payments shall accrue interest at 1.5% per month."
3.3  SLA Uptime Target (Article 5.1) — HIGH

The Term Sheet guarantees 99.95% monthly uptime. The draft MSA reduces this to 99.9%. The difference of 0.05% translates to approximately 22 additional minutes of permissible downtime per month. For mission-critical cloud infrastructure, this dilution of the uptime commitment materially weakens Client's operational protections. Additionally, the service-credit formula in Section 5.2 of the MSA caps credits at 20% of the monthly fee, whereas the Term Sheet allows up to 30%.
Recommended language:

"Provider guarantees a monthly uptime of 99.95% for all production environments. Service credits shall equal 5% of the monthly fee for each 0.01% below 99.95%, up to a maximum credit of 30% of the monthly fee."
3.4  SOC 2 Type II Compliance (Article 6) — HIGH

The Term Sheet explicitly requires Provider to maintain SOC 2 Type II certification and furnish annual audit reports. The draft MSA contains no reference to SOC 2 compliance; Section 6.3 instead uses vague "commercially reasonable security controls" language. This omission exposes Client to significant compliance risk, particularly if Client is subject to regulatory requirements that depend on vendor SOC 2 attestation.
Recommended language:

"Provider shall maintain SOC 2 Type II certification throughout the Term and shall provide Client with a copy of its annual SOC 2 Type II audit report within thirty (30) days of the report's issuance. Provider shall promptly notify Client of any material findings or qualifications in such report."
3.5  Intellectual Property Ownership (Article 7.1) — CRITICAL

This is the most consequential discrepancy. The Term Sheet provides that Client owns all custom work product. The draft MSA reverses this entirely: Provider retains ownership of all materials (including newly created work), granting Client only a non-exclusive license. This would leave Client without ownership of custom configurations and automation built specifically for its environment, creating vendor lock-in and jeopardizing Client's ability to transition to another provider.
Recommended language:

"All custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, and derivative works created by Provider specifically for Client under this Agreement ("Custom Work Product") shall be the exclusive property of Client. Provider retains ownership of its pre-existing tools, frameworks, and methodologies."
3.6  Liability Cap (Article 8.1) — HIGH

The Term Sheet sets the liability cap at twelve (12) months of fees ($570,000). The draft MSA reduces this to six (6) months ($285,000 based on the MSA's lower fee figure, or $270,000 using the correct fee). Halving the liability cap substantially limits Client's recourse in the event of a significant service failure or data breach. The Term Sheet also carves out confidentiality breaches, willful misconduct, and third-party IP claims from the cap — the MSA only carves out confidentiality.
Recommended language:

"Each party's aggregate liability under this Agreement shall not exceed twelve (12) months of fees paid or payable hereunder. This cap shall not apply to breaches of confidentiality obligations, willful misconduct, or indemnification obligations arising from third-party intellectual property claims."
3.7  Termination Notice Period (Article 9.1) — MEDIUM

The Term Sheet requires 90 days' notice for termination for convenience. The draft MSA shortens this to 60 days. While a shorter notice period gives both parties more flexibility, it reduces Client's transition runway if Provider terminates. Given the complexity of cloud infrastructure migration, 90 days is more appropriate.
Recommended language:
"Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience by providing ninety (90) days' prior written notice to the other party."
3.8  Governing Law (Article 10) — MEDIUM

The Term Sheet designates Delaware as the governing jurisdiction, consistent with Client's state of incorporation. The draft MSA substitutes New York. While both jurisdictions have well-developed commercial law, the change was not negotiated and would require Client to litigate or arbitrate disputes in a different forum. This should be corrected to maintain consistency with the Term Sheet and Client's corporate nexus.
Recommended language:

"This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware. Any dispute shall be resolved by binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware under the rules of the American Arbitration Association."
3.9  Auto-Renewal Clause (Article 3.2) — LOW

The draft MSA introduces an automatic renewal provision for successive one-year terms. The Term Sheet contains no such provision — it contemplates a fixed three-year term. While auto-renewal clauses are common in services agreements, this term was not discussed or agreed upon. Client should either delete the clause entirely or negotiate acceptable renewal terms with adequate notice provisions.
Recommended action:
Delete Section 3.2 in its entirety, or revise to require mutual written agreement to renew.
4. Risk Assessment
Overall Risk Rating: HIGH

The draft MSA contains multiple material deviations from the executed Term Sheet. Three discrepancies — the monthly fee, IP ownership, and payment terms — are classified as Critical because they directly affect Client's financial exposure and proprietary rights. The cumulative effect of all nine discrepancies significantly shifts the risk balance in Provider's favor. The MSA should not be executed in its current form.

Of particular concern is the IP ownership reversal (Section 7.1). If executed as drafted, Client would lose ownership of all custom work product developed during the engagement, creating substantial vendor lock-in and undermining Client's negotiating position in any future re-procurement.
5. Recommended Next Steps
We recommend the following prioritized actions:
1.  Immediate Redline — Prepare a marked-up version of the MSA correcting all nine discrepancies identified above. Priority should be given to the three Critical items (fee, payment terms, IP).

2.  Provider Discussion — Schedule a call with Stratos's legal team within the next five business days to review the redline and confirm whether the discrepancies were drafting errors or intentional deviations.
3.  SOC 2 Verification — Request a copy of Provider's current SOC 2 Type II report before executing the MSA to confirm certification status.
4.  Auto-Renewal Negotiation — Determine Client's position on auto-renewal and, if acceptable in principle, negotiate a mutual opt-in renewal mechanism with at least 90 days' advance notice.
5.  Final Review — Once the redline is accepted, conduct a final review of the revised MSA to ensure all changes have been accurately incorporated before routing for signature.
Please do not hesitate to contact us if you have questions or wish to discuss any of the above findings in greater detail.

rows: Rewrite to assign IP to Client

content.text: PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT
MEMORANDUM
TO: Catherine A. Morales, Chief Operating Officer, Apex Dynamics Inc.
FROM: Outside Counsel — Contract Review Team
DATE: March 15, 2026
RE: Review of Draft Master Services Agreement (v2) — Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC — Discrepancy Analysis Against Executed Term Sheet
1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

We have completed a detailed comparison of the fully executed Term Sheet dated March 1, 2026 (the "Term Sheet") against the draft Master Services Agreement, Version 2 (the "Draft MSA") circulated by counsel for Stratos Cloud Solutions LLC ("Provider"). Our review has identified eighteen (18) material discrepancies between the two documents, of which seven (7) are classified as Critical, six (6) as High, three (3) as Medium, and two (2) as Low severity. The most consequential departures involve the monthly service fee (a $2,500/month reduction favoring Provider), the liability cap (reduced from twelve to six months of fees), intellectual property ownership of custom work product (shifted entirely to Provider), and a change in governing law from Delaware to New York. We strongly recommend that Apex Dynamics withhold execution of the Draft MSA until all Critical and High severity discrepancies are resolved through redline negotiation with Provider's counsel.
2. DISCREPANCY TABLE
3. DETAILED ANALYSIS
Discrepancy 1 — Monthly Service Fee (Art. 4.1) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: $47,500.00/month | Draft MSA: $45,000.00/month

Legal and Business Impact: The Draft MSA understates the agreed monthly fee by $2,500.00, resulting in a total difference of $90,000.00 over a three-year term. While on its face the lower fee appears to benefit Client, this discrepancy raises serious concerns. If the fee reduction is intentional on Provider's part, it may signal an attempt to justify reduced service scope, lower SLA commitments, or other concessions embedded elsewhere in the MSA. If unintentional, it reflects a lack of drafting discipline that warrants scrutiny of the entire document. Either way, the MSA must reflect the fee the parties bargained for.
Recommended Language:
> "Client shall pay Provider a monthly service fee of Forty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($47,500.00), payable in arrears."
Discrepancy 2 — Payment Terms (Art. 4.2) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: Net 30 days | Draft MSA: Net 45 days

Legal and Business Impact: Extending the payment window from 30 to 45 days provides Provider with an additional 15 days of float on each invoice cycle, which over a three-year engagement represents meaningful working capital advantage. This departure from the Term Sheet was not negotiated and should be corrected.
Recommended Language:

> "Invoices are due and payable within thirty (30) days of Client's receipt of a valid invoice ('Net 30'). Late payments shall accrue interest at the lesser of 1.5% per month or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law."
Discrepancy 3 — Unilateral Fee Escalation (Art. 4.3) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: No fee escalation provision | Draft MSA: Provider may increase fees up to 5% annually on 60 days' notice

Legal and Business Impact: This is an entirely new provision absent from the Term Sheet. A 5% annual escalation could increase the monthly fee to approximately $55,000 by Year 3, representing a potential cumulative overpayment of more than $135,000 relative to the fixed fee agreed upon. Unilateral escalation rights fundamentally alter the financial bargain struck in the Term Sheet.
Recommended Language:

> Delete Section 4.3 in its entirety. If a fee adjustment mechanism is desired, replace with: "Any adjustment to fees shall require the mutual written agreement of both parties, memorialized in a written amendment to this Agreement."
Discrepancy 4 — Uptime SLA (Art. 5.1) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: 99.95% monthly uptime | Draft MSA: 99.9% monthly uptime

Legal and Business Impact: The difference between 99.95% and 99.9% uptime is significant in practice. At 99.95%, Provider commits to no more than approximately 21.9 minutes of downtime per month; at 99.9%, the permitted downtime doubles to approximately 43.8 minutes. For a mission-critical cloud infrastructure engagement, this 0.05% degradation can translate to material business disruption and revenue loss for Client.
Recommended Language:
> "Provider guarantees a monthly uptime of 99.95% for all production environments."
Discrepancy 5 — SLA Credit Calculation Granularity (Art. 5.2) — HIGH
Term Sheet: 5% credit per 0.01% shortfall below 99.95% | Draft MSA: 5% credit per 0.05% shortfall below target

Legal and Business Impact: Under the Term Sheet, a 0.05% shortfall would generate a 25% credit (5 × 5%); under the Draft MSA, the same shortfall generates only a 5% credit. This fivefold reduction in credit sensitivity dramatically weakens Client's contractual incentive mechanism for Provider performance.
Recommended Language:

> "Client shall be entitled to service credits equal to five percent (5%) of the monthly service fee for each one-hundredth of one percent (0.01%) that Provider's actual uptime falls below 99.95%, up to the maximum credit set forth herein."
Discrepancy 6 — Maximum SLA Credit Cap (Art. 5.2) — HIGH
Term Sheet: 30% of monthly fee | Draft MSA: 20% of monthly fee

Legal and Business Impact: Reducing the maximum credit from 30% to 20% limits Client's recourse during severe outage events. Under the Term Sheet formula, a credit cap of 30% would be reached at approximately 99.89% uptime—a meaningful threshold. Lowering the cap to 20% reduces Client's financial protection during the most impactful service failures.
Recommended Language:
> "...up to a maximum credit of thirty percent (30%) of the monthly service fee in any given month."
Discrepancy 7 — Sole and Exclusive Remedy (Art. 5.3) — HIGH
Term Sheet: No exclusive remedy limitation | Draft MSA: Service credits are Client's sole and exclusive remedy for SLA failures

Legal and Business Impact: This new provision would bar Client from pursuing termination rights, damages, or other contractual remedies in the event of chronic or catastrophic service failures. It effectively immunizes Provider from liability beyond modest service credits, even if prolonged outages cause substantial harm.
Recommended Language:

> "Service credits shall be without prejudice to Client's other rights and remedies under this Agreement, at law, or in equity, including the right to terminate this Agreement pursuant to Article 9 in the event of chronic or material SLA failures. For purposes of this Section, 'chronic SLA failure' means Provider's failure to meet the uptime target in any three (3) out of six (6) consecutive calendar months."
Discrepancy 8 — RPO and RTO Specificity (Art. 2.1(d)) — HIGH
Term Sheet: RPO of 1 hour, RTO of 4 hours | Draft MSA: "Agreed RPO and RTO targets" (undefined)

Legal and Business Impact: The Draft MSA's vague reference to "agreed" targets renders the disaster recovery commitment unenforceable. Without express RPO and RTO values, Provider has no measurable obligation, and Client has no basis for asserting a breach in the event of a disaster recovery failure.
Recommended Language:
> "(d) disaster recovery architecture with a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of one (1) hour and a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of four (4) hours;"
Discrepancy 9 — SOC 2 Type II Certification and Audit Rights (Art. 6) — HIGH

Term Sheet: SOC 2 Type II required throughout the Term; annual audit reports within 30 days of issuance | Draft MSA: No mention of SOC 2 or audit reports; only "commercially reasonable security controls"

Legal and Business Impact: The omission of the SOC 2 Type II requirement is a significant compliance gap. SOC 2 Type II certification provides independent third-party assurance of Provider's control environment. "Commercially reasonable security controls" is a subjective standard that provides Client with substantially less protection and no verifiable benchmark.
Recommended Language:

> Add new Section 6.5: "Provider shall maintain SOC 2 Type II certification from a nationally recognized audit firm throughout the Term and shall deliver a copy of each annual SOC 2 Type II audit report to Client within thirty (30) days of the report's issuance. Provider shall promptly notify Client of any material findings or qualifications contained in such reports."
Discrepancy 10 — Intellectual Property Ownership of Custom Work Product (Art. 7.1) — CRITICAL

Term Sheet: Custom Work Product (custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, and derivative works created specifically for Client) is Client's exclusive property; Provider retains only pre-existing tools, frameworks, and methodologies | Draft MSA: Provider retains all IP, including newly created work; Client receives only a non-exclusive, perpetual license

Legal and Business Impact: This is one of the most consequential discrepancies in the Draft MSA. Under the Term Sheet, Client owns all bespoke work product. Under the Draft MSA, Provider would own everything and could license the same materials to Client's competitors. This reversal of IP ownership fundamentally undermines Client's investment in customized infrastructure solutions and creates vendor lock-in risk. It also raises concerns about Client's ability to transition to another provider upon termination, as Client would lack ownership of critical operational tooling.
Recommended Language:

> "7.1 Custom Work Product. All custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, and derivative works created by Provider specifically for Client under this Agreement ('Custom Work Product') shall be the exclusive property of Client. Provider hereby assigns and agrees to assign all right, title, and interest in such Custom Work Product to Client. Provider shall execute all documents and take all actions reasonably necessary to effectuate such assignment.

> 7.2 Provider Pre-Existing IP. Provider retains all right, title, and interest in its pre-existing tools, frameworks, and methodologies ('Provider IP'). To the extent any Provider IP is incorporated into Custom Work Product, Provider grants Client a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license to use such Provider IP solely in connection with Client's use of the Custom Work Product.
> 7.3 Client IP. Client retains all right, title, and interest in Client's pre-existing intellectual property and data."
Discrepancy 11 — Liability Cap Amount (Art. 8.1) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: 12 months of fees paid or payable | Draft MSA: 6 months of fees paid or payable

Legal and Business Impact: The Draft MSA reduces the liability cap by 50%, from approximately $570,000 (12 × $47,500) to approximately $285,000 (6 × $47,500). Given the critical nature of managed cloud infrastructure services and the potential magnitude of losses from a catastrophic failure (including business interruption, data loss, and regulatory exposure), a 6-month cap is commercially unreasonable and inconsistent with the agreed-upon allocation of risk.
Recommended Language:
> "NEITHER PARTY'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT SHALL EXCEED AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO TWELVE (12) MONTHS OF FEES PAID OR PAYABLE HEREUNDER (THE 'LIABILITY CAP')."
Discrepancy 12 — Liability Cap Carve-Outs (Art. 8.1) — HIGH
Term Sheet: Carve-outs for (i) confidentiality breaches, (ii) willful misconduct, and (iii) third-party IP indemnification | Draft MSA: Carve-out only for confidentiality breaches

Legal and Business Impact: Removing the willful misconduct and IP indemnification carve-outs allows Provider to limit its exposure even for intentional wrongdoing and IP infringement — outcomes that are plainly unreasonable and may not be enforceable in some jurisdictions.
Recommended Language:

> "THE LIABILITY CAP SHALL NOT APPLY TO: (I) BREACHES OF ARTICLE 6 (CONFIDENTIALITY AND DATA PROTECTION); (II) A PARTY'S WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR GROSS NEGLIGENCE; OR (III) EITHER PARTY'S INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM THIRD-PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY CLAIMS."
Discrepancy 13 — Consequential Damages Exclusion (Art. 8.2) — HIGH
Term Sheet: No consequential damages exclusion | Draft MSA: Full mutual exclusion of indirect, incidental, special, consequential, and punitive damages

Legal and Business Impact: This is an entirely new provision not contemplated by the Term Sheet. A blanket consequential damages waiver could bar Client from recovering lost profits, business interruption damages, and regulatory fines resulting from Provider's failures — precisely the types of damages most likely to arise from a managed infrastructure service failure.
Recommended Language:

> "THE EXCLUSION OF CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.2 SHALL NOT APPLY TO: (I) BREACHES OF ARTICLE 6 (CONFIDENTIALITY AND DATA PROTECTION); (II) A PARTY'S WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR GROSS NEGLIGENCE; (III) EITHER PARTY'S INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM THIRD-PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY CLAIMS; OR (IV) PROVIDER'S BREACH OF THE DATA HANDLING AND SECURITY OBLIGATIONS SET FORTH IN THIS AGREEMENT."
Discrepancy 14 — Termination for Convenience Notice Period (Art. 9.1) — MEDIUM
Term Sheet: 90 days' prior written notice | Draft MSA: 60 days' prior written notice

Legal and Business Impact: A shorter notice period could disadvantage Client if Provider elects to terminate during a critical business period, as 60 days may be insufficient for Client to procure and transition to an alternative service provider for mission-critical cloud infrastructure.
Recommended Language:
> "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience by providing ninety (90) days' prior written notice to the other party."
Discrepancy 15 — Auto-Renewal (Art. 3.2) — MEDIUM
Term Sheet: Fixed 3-year term expiring February 28, 2029; no auto-renewal | Draft MSA: Auto-renewal for successive 1-year periods with 60-day non-renewal notice

Legal and Business Impact: Auto-renewal provisions create a risk of inadvertent contract extension if the non-renewal notice deadline is missed. This is particularly problematic where, as here, the Term Sheet contemplated a definite expiration date. The 60-day non-renewal notice window is relatively short and may be missed by Client's administrative processes.
Recommended Language:

> "This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for a period of three (3) years, expiring on February 28, 2029, unless earlier terminated in accordance with Article 9. The parties may extend this Agreement beyond the Initial Term only by mutual written agreement executed by authorized representatives of both parties."
Discrepancy 16 — Governing Law (Art. 10.1) — CRITICAL
Term Sheet: Delaware law | Draft MSA: New York law

Legal and Business Impact: Apex Dynamics is a Delaware corporation, and the Term Sheet expressly selected Delaware law. New York law differs from Delaware law in several respects material to commercial contracts, including the interpretation of indemnification provisions, limitation of liability clauses, and the enforceability of certain fee-shifting mechanisms. Changing the governing law to New York could alter the parties' substantive rights and was not agreed upon.
Recommended Language:
> "This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles."
Discrepancy 17 — Dispute Resolution Venue and Procedure (Art. 10.2) — HIGH
Term Sheet: Binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware (AAA rules) | Draft MSA: Mandatory mediation, then arbitration in New York, New York (AAA rules)

Legal and Business Impact: The venue change from Wilmington, DE to New York, NY imposes additional travel and logistical costs on Client, whose principal office is in Wilmington. The addition of a mandatory mediation step (absent from the Term Sheet) introduces potential delay of 60+ days before arbitration can commence. While mediation can be beneficial, making it mandatory and a condition precedent to arbitration was not part of the parties' agreement.
Recommended Language:

> "Any dispute arising under this Agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The parties may, but shall not be required to, submit any dispute to non-binding mediation prior to initiating arbitration."
Discrepancy 18 — Quarterly Business Review Specificity (Art. 2.1(e)) — LOW
Term Sheet: Quarterly business reviews with Client's IT leadership team | Draft MSA: "Quarterly business reviews" (generic)
Legal and Business Impact: Minor but potentially meaningful for ensuring that Provider commits to senior-level engagement at QBRs.
Recommended Language:
> "(e) quarterly business reviews with Client's IT leadership team, including at a minimum a review of service performance metrics, capacity planning, and strategic technology roadmap alignment."
Additional Observation — Entire Agreement / Merger Clause (Art. 11.1)
Term Sheet: N/A | Draft MSA: The Agreement "supersedes all prior or contemporaneous understandings"

Legal and Business Impact: Article 11.1 of the Draft MSA contains a standard merger clause that, upon execution, would supersede the Term Sheet. This is not itself a discrepancy, but it underscores the critical importance of ensuring that all Term Sheet provisions are accurately reflected in the MSA before execution. Once the MSA is signed, the Term Sheet will have no independent legal force, and Client will be unable to rely on it to resolve ambiguities in the MSA.
Recommended Action: Ensure all Term Sheet terms are incorporated into the MSA prior to execution. Consider adding a recital referencing the Term Sheet as reflecting the parties' intent.
4. RISK ASSESSMENT
Overall Risk Rating: HIGH

Justification: The Draft MSA contains seven Critical-severity discrepancies and six High-severity discrepancies that, taken together, represent a systematic reallocation of risk, cost, and rights from Provider to Client. The cumulative effect of these departures — including a reduced fee (potentially offered as a trade for weakened terms), halved liability cap, reversed IP ownership, diluted SLA protections, elimination of compliance requirements, and a change in governing law — would place Apex Dynamics in a materially worse position than the Term Sheet contemplated. The pattern suggests these changes were deliberate rather than inadvertent, which warrants a firm negotiating posture. Executing the MSA in its current form would expose Client to significant financial, operational, legal, and compliance risk.
5. RECOMMENDED NEXT STEPS
The following action items are listed in order of priority:

**Immediately reject the Draft MSA in its current form** and notify Provider's counsel that the draft contains material deviations from the executed Term Sheet that must be resolved before Apex Dynamics will consider execution.

**Prepare and circulate a comprehensive redline** of the Draft MSA incorporating all corrections identified in this memorandum, with particular emphasis on the seven Critical-severity items (fee, payment terms, fee escalation, uptime SLA, IP ownership, liability cap, and governing law).

**Demand written confirmation from Provider** that the executed Term Sheet governs the parties' relationship pending MSA execution, and that Provider will not take any action inconsistent with the Term Sheet during the negotiation period.

**Negotiate the High-severity items** as a package, prioritizing: (a) restoration of SLA credit terms (granularity, cap, and removal of exclusive remedy); (b) reinstatement of SOC 2 Type II and audit requirements; (c) restoration of all liability cap carve-outs; (d) narrowing or carving out exceptions to the consequential damages exclusion; and (e) correction of dispute resolution venue to Wilmington, Delaware.

**Address Medium and Low-severity items** in the same redline, including restoration of the 90-day termination notice period, deletion or modification of the auto-renewal provision, and specification of QBR attendees.
**Conduct a follow-up review** of any revised MSA draft circulated by Provider to confirm all discrepancies have been resolved and no new adverse terms have been introduced.
**Consider engaging local Delaware counsel** to provide a supplemental analysis if Provider insists on New York governing law, to assess the practical impact on enforceability of key provisions.

This memorandum is prepared as attorney work product and is protected by the attorney-client privilege. It is intended solely for the use of Apex Dynamics Inc. and should not be disclosed to any third party without the prior written consent of counsel.

content.text: Contract Review Memo
To: Apex Dynamics Inc.
From: Outside Counsel
Date: March 17, 2026
Re: Comparison of Executed Term Sheet and Provider Draft Master Services Agreement (v2) — Managed Cloud Infrastructure Services
1. Executive Summary

We compared the executed Term Sheet dated March 1, 2026 against Provider's draft Master Services Agreement v2 and identified 11 material discrepancies between the agreed commercial/legal terms and the draft MSA. Several of these deviations are significant and favor Provider, including changes to fees, payment timing, service levels, intellectual property ownership, liability allocation, governing law, dispute venue, and automatic renewal. Overall, the draft MSA does not accurately reflect the executed Term Sheet and should not be signed in its current form. The most serious issues are the reversals of agreed ownership and risk-allocation provisions, together with dilution of service commitments and introduction of unilateral economics not present in the signed deal.
2. Discrepancy Table
3. Detailed Analysis
3.1 Scope of Services / Disaster Recovery Metrics
Relevant MSA Section: Article 2.1
Term Sheet Position: Provider must deliver disaster recovery architecture with RPO of 1 hour and RTO of 4 hours, plus quarterly business reviews with Client's IT leadership team.
Draft MSA Position: Disaster recovery commitment is diluted to architecture with "agreed RPO and RTO targets," and the business review obligation no longer specifies Client's IT leadership team.

Impact: This is a meaningful reduction in service specificity. By replacing concrete disaster recovery metrics with open-ended future agreement, Provider preserves flexibility to offer weaker continuity commitments or argue that no enforceable recovery target exists absent later documentation. The omission of the specific QBR audience similarly weakens governance and executive oversight commitments. For managed infrastructure services, DR metrics are central operational protections and should not be left ambiguous.
Recommended Action: Reinstate the exact operational metrics from the Term Sheet and preserve the governance cadence.
Recommended Language:

"Provider shall deliver managed cloud infrastructure services, including disaster recovery architecture with a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of one (1) hour and a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of four (4) hours, and quarterly business reviews with Client's IT leadership team."
3.2 Term / Automatic Renewal
Relevant MSA Section: Article 3.2
Term Sheet Position: Agreement runs for three years, expiring February 28, 2029, unless earlier terminated.
Draft MSA Position: Adds automatic renewal for successive one-year periods unless either party gives 60 days' notice of non-renewal.

Impact: The auto-renewal provision materially changes the commercial bargain. The signed Term Sheet establishes a fixed term with a defined expiration date; the draft MSA instead creates a continuing relationship unless Client affirmatively opts out. This can create unwanted spend, reduce renegotiation leverage, and generate administrative risk if notice is missed.
Recommended Action: Delete automatic renewal and require mutual written agreement for any extension.
Recommended Language:

"This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and continue through February 28, 2029, unless earlier terminated in accordance with this Agreement. Any extension or renewal shall require the parties' mutual written agreement."
3.3 Fees, Payment Terms, and Fee Increases
Relevant MSA Sections: Articles 4.1-4.3
Term Sheet Position: Monthly fee of $47,500, payable in arrears; Net 30 from receipt of valid invoice; late payments at 1.5% per month.
Draft MSA Position: Monthly fee changed to $45,000; payment extended to Net 45; Provider may unilaterally increase fees up to 5% per annum.

Impact: This article does not align with the executed economics. Although the lower monthly fee is nominally favorable to Client, it is still a deviation from the signed commercial agreement and may indicate that the draft is not tied to the executed term sheet. More importantly, the draft introduces a unilateral fee escalation mechanism that was not agreed and materially undermines price certainty over the initial term. The payment term deviation is also inconsistent with the bargain and should be corrected for internal controls alignment.
Recommended Action: Conform all financial terms to the Term Sheet and delete unilateral annual fee increases.
Recommended Language:

"Client shall pay Provider a monthly service fee of Forty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($47,500.00), payable in arrears. Invoices are due and payable within thirty (30) days after Client's receipt of a valid invoice. Late payments shall accrue interest at 1.5% per month. Provider shall have no right to increase fees during the Term except as expressly agreed in a written amendment signed by both parties."
3.4 Service Levels and Remedies
Relevant MSA Sections: Articles 5.1-5.3
Term Sheet Position: 99.95% monthly uptime; service credits of 5% of the monthly fee for each 0.01% below 99.95%, up to 30% of monthly fee.
Draft MSA Position: Reduces uptime to 99.9%; credits only for each 0.05% shortfall; lowers cap to 20%; adds that service credits are Client's sole and exclusive remedy.

Impact: This is a major degradation of the SLA package. Lower uptime tolerance and less favorable credit mechanics materially reduce Provider accountability for outages. The sole-and-exclusive-remedy clause further prevents Client from pursuing damages or other remedies for repeated downtime, potentially even where outages cause substantial operational harm. For infrastructure services supporting production environments, this is a significant commercial and operational risk.
Recommended Action: Restore the Term Sheet SLA metrics and reject the sole-remedy limitation unless a broader service governance/risk package is negotiated.
Recommended Language:

"Provider guarantees a monthly uptime of 99.95% for all production environments. Failure to meet this target entitles Client to service credits equal to 5% of the monthly service fee for each 0.01% below 99.95%, up to a maximum credit of 30% of the monthly service fee."
If Provider insists on a remedies clause, add:
"Service credits shall be in addition to, and not in lieu of, any other rights or remedies available to Client under this Agreement or at law for material or repeated service failures."
3.5 Data Handling / Compliance Omissions
Relevant MSA Section: Article 6

Term Sheet Position: Provider must maintain SOC 2 Type II certification throughout the Term and provide annual audit reports within 30 days of issuance; must encrypt data and keep data within the continental U.S. absent consent.
Draft MSA Position: Preserves encryption and data localization restriction, but omits the SOC 2 Type II maintenance covenant and annual audit report delivery requirement.

Impact: The omitted compliance obligations remove a concrete, auditable security baseline that Client bargained for. "Commercially reasonable security controls" is a softer and more subjective standard, making enforcement more difficult and reducing assurance for internal audit, customer commitments, and regulatory/compliance posture. For a managed cloud provider with broad access to infrastructure and data, SOC 2 Type II is a key diligence and oversight requirement.
Recommended Action: Add the omitted certification and reporting language without qualification.
Recommended Language:
"Provider shall maintain SOC 2 Type II certification throughout the Term and shall provide Client with its annual SOC 2 Type II audit report within thirty (30) days after issuance."
3.6 Intellectual Property Ownership Reversal
Relevant MSA Section: Article 7.1

Term Sheet Position: All custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, and derivative works created specifically for Client are the exclusive property of Client; Provider retains ownership of pre-existing tools, frameworks, and methodologies.

Draft MSA Position: Provider retains ownership of tools, scripts, configurations, and methodologies developed in connection with the Services, whether pre-existing or newly created; Client receives only a non-exclusive perpetual internal-use license.

Impact: This is the most significant substantive departure in the draft MSA. It reverses ownership of custom work product that the Term Sheet expressly awarded to Client. If accepted, Client could lose exclusive control over bespoke configurations and automation developed for its environment, weakening portability, increasing switching costs, and allowing Provider to reuse Client-funded work for other customers. It also reduces Client's leverage in any transition or termination scenario.

Recommended Action: Replace Article 7.1 with a clear custom-work-product ownership clause consistent with the Term Sheet and define a limited license back to Provider only if needed to perform services.
Recommended Language:

"All custom configurations, scripts, automation tooling, deliverables, and derivative works created by or for Provider specifically for Client in connection with the Services (collectively, 'Custom Work Product') shall be the exclusive property of Client. To the extent any such Custom Work Product does not automatically vest in Client, Provider hereby assigns to Client all right, title, and interest therein. Provider retains all right, title, and interest in its pre-existing tools, frameworks, methodologies, and other materials developed independently of the Services, together with any generic know-how not incorporating Client Confidential Information or Client-specific deliverables."
3.7 Limitation of Liability
Relevant MSA Section: Article 8.1

Term Sheet Position: Aggregate liability cap of 12 months of fees paid or payable; cap does not apply to confidentiality breaches, willful misconduct, or indemnification obligations arising from third-party IP claims.
Draft MSA Position: Liability cap reduced to 6 months of fees; only carve-out is for breaches of confidentiality.

Impact: This is a material deterioration in Client's risk allocation. Halving the cap significantly limits recovery for service failures, security events, transition failures, and other breaches. The omission of carve-outs for willful misconduct and IP indemnity is especially problematic: it would cap Provider's liability even for intentional wrongdoing or third-party infringement claims arising from Provider's materials/services. That is a substantial legal and business risk.
Recommended Action: Restore the agreed cap and carve-outs in full.
Recommended Language:

"Each party's aggregate liability under this Agreement shall not exceed an amount equal to twelve (12) months of fees paid or payable under this Agreement; provided, however, that this limitation shall not apply to (i) breaches of confidentiality obligations, (ii) willful misconduct, or (iii) indemnification obligations arising from third-party intellectual property claims."
3.8 Consequential Damages Waiver
Relevant MSA Section: Article 8.2
Term Sheet Position: No express consequential damages waiver.
Draft MSA Position: Broad mutual exclusion of indirect, incidental, special, consequential, and punitive damages.

Impact: Although common in commercial agreements, this clause is an additional limitation of liability not reflected in the Term Sheet. Depending on applicable law and claim type, it may bar recovery for categories of loss that can be significant in a cloud outage, migration failure, or security incident, including costs tied to business interruption, restoration, and downstream customer impacts. If accepted, the clause should be carefully qualified to preserve meaningful remedies for core risk areas.
Recommended Action: Either delete Article 8.2 as non-agreed or add exceptions for key claims.
Recommended Language:

"The exclusions in this Section shall not apply to damages arising from breaches of confidentiality, data security incidents caused by Provider's breach, willful misconduct, gross negligence, infringement or indemnification claims, or Provider's failure to comply with its data handling and location obligations."
3.9 Termination for Convenience Notice Period
Relevant MSA Section: Article 9.1
Term Sheet Position: Either party may terminate for convenience on 90 days' prior written notice.
Draft MSA Position: Reduces notice period to 60 days.

Impact: A shorter convenience termination notice period reduces Client's time to prepare internal transition plans, secure replacement support, and manage service continuity. In a managed infrastructure context, longer notice is operationally valuable, especially where migration and handoff can take substantial time.
Recommended Action: Restore 90 days.
Recommended Language:
"Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to the other party."
3.10 Transition Assistance at Client's Expense
Relevant MSA Section: Article 9.3
Term Sheet Position: Silent.
Draft MSA Position: Provider must cooperate in transition for up to 90 days, at Client's expense.

Impact: This is a new term not addressed in the Term Sheet. Transition support is helpful, but the open-ended statement that assistance is at Client's expense creates cost uncertainty and may enable Provider to charge premium rates at the point of exit, when Client has reduced leverage. If retained, pricing, scope, and service standards should be defined.

Recommended Action: Clarify that reasonable transition assistance is included in fees through the termination effective date, and any post-termination services are subject to pre-agreed rates and a written transition plan.
Recommended Language:

"Provider shall provide reasonable transition assistance for up to ninety (90) days following notice of termination or expiration. Such assistance shall be included in the fees payable through the effective termination/expiration date, and any additional post-termination transition services shall be provided only pursuant to a mutually agreed written transition plan at the rates set forth in Exhibit [X], with total transition fees not to exceed [cap]."
3.11 Governing Law and Dispute Resolution
Relevant MSA Sections: Articles 10.1-10.2
Term Sheet Position: Delaware law; binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware under AAA rules.
Draft MSA Position: New York law; mandatory pre-arbitration mediation; arbitration in New York, New York.

Impact: This is a direct contradiction of the signed Term Sheet. Changing governing law and venue can materially affect litigation strategy, cost, convenience, and interpretive outcomes. Mandatory mediation also adds time and process before final dispute resolution, which may be undesirable in urgent service or security disputes. Given Client is a Delaware corporation and the parties already agreed Delaware/Wilmington, the draft should be conformed.

Recommended Action: Restore Delaware law and Wilmington arbitration. If mediation is commercially desirable, make it optional or allow either party to bypass it for injunctive or urgent operational relief.
Recommended Language:

"This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. Any dispute arising under this Agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware under the rules of the American Arbitration Association."
4. Risk Assessment
Overall Risk Rating:** **High

Justification: The draft MSA contains multiple departures from the executed Term Sheet, including several Critical discrepancies affecting core deal terms, financial certainty, service performance, IP ownership, and liability allocation. Most notably, the MSA reverses ownership of custom work product, reduces the liability cap while narrowing carve-outs, weakens SLA commitments, and changes governing law/venue. These are not drafting cleanups; they are substantive shifts in economic and legal risk toward Client. The agreement should be revised comprehensively before execution.
5. Recommended Next Steps
**Do not sign the draft MSA in its current form.** The current draft does not accurately implement the executed Term Sheet.

**Send a redline conforming the MSA to the Term Sheet.** Priority changes should address: (i) IP ownership, (ii) liability cap/carve-outs, (iii) SLA metrics/remedies, (iv) fee escalation deletion, and (v) Delaware law/venue.
**Require express insertion of omitted compliance terms.** Add SOC 2 Type II maintenance and annual audit report delivery obligations.
**Remove non-agreed Provider-favorable additions.** Delete automatic renewal, unilateral annual fee increases, sole/exclusive SLA remedy language, and New York governing law/venue.
**Clarify transition assistance economics.** If Article 9.3 is retained, fix rates, scope, timing, and a fee cap to avoid exit-cost leverage.

**Confirm whether any additional deal points are intended.** The lower monthly fee in the draft suggests the MSA may have been prepared from an inconsistent internal precedent or outdated business markup; request Provider to confirm that the executed Term Sheet governs all commercial points.

**Prepare fallback positions for negotiation.** If Provider resists, possible secondary compromises include optional mediation, limited transition assistance fees at pre-agreed rates, and a qualified consequential damages waiver with robust carve-outs.
6. Conclusion

The Provider draft MSA v2 should be treated as a non-conforming draft that requires material revision. The executed Term Sheet provides a clear benchmark, and the final MSA should be brought into alignment with those agreed terms before the parties proceed to signature.
